but the great volume of water SEEM
ed to have little effect on the roar
ing flames. The water supply was
poor most of the time. The big
steamer from the central station.
however, was able to get plenty of
water by dropping it's suction into
the Sawmill Phoenix dam.


At s o'clock a large portion of the
building collapsed and fell into the
river. From that time on the fire
was under control. From the nature
of the buildings it was a particularly
hard fire to fight. They were covered
with iron sheeting and the fremen
had no way to get at the flames ex-
cept through the doors and windows.


The Washington Water Power COM
pany purchased the mill in 1889.
There were over 10000 bushels of
grain and 500 barrels of flour in the
mill and these were wholly consumed.


1t required all the energy of the
employees of the Water Power COM
pany to save the new street car
bridge a bucket brigade kept 1t wet
throughout the confla grafton or il
would probably have gone with the
mill. All of the books of the mill
company were saved and removed to
the Echo mill.
